enrichment.short_description: Change Healthcare is a major US healthcare technology company providing claims processing, payment, and data connectivity between payers, providers, and pharmacies. Now part of Optum Insight (UnitedHealth Group), it processes a huge share of the country's medical claims and was the target of a severe February 2024 ransomware attack that disrupted US healthcare billing nationwide.
enrichment.pricing_model: Enterprise / custom quote (B2B healthcare infrastructure, not self-serve SaaS)
enrichment.seo_title: Change Healthcare Review 2026 - Features, Ownership, and the 2024 Cyberattack
enrichment.seo_meta_description: An in-depth, factual look at Change Healthcare: what it does, its history, its acquisition by Optum/UnitedHealth, and the 2024 ransomware attack that disrupted US healthcare claims.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Change Healthcare is one of the largest healthcare technology and data connectivity companies in the United States, sitting at the center of the country's medical claims, billing, and payment infrastructure. Its clearinghouse and revenue-cycle-management tools connect hospitals, physician practices, pharmacies, laboratories, and insurance payers, historically routing roughly half of all US medical claims and a large share of pharmacy and eligibility transactions through its systems. The modern company traces its roots to Emdeon (originally spun out of WebMD's transaction-services business in 2007), which was renamed Change Healthcare after a 2015 rebrand, and then combined with McKesson Corporation's health IT unit in 2017 under the Change Healthcare name, with McKesson holding a majority stake. Change Healthcare went public via IPO in 2019 before being acquired by Optum, the health-services arm of UnitedHealth Group, in a roughly 13 billion dollar all-cash deal that closed in October 2022 after a Department of Justice antitrust challenge was rejected in court. Since the acquisition, Change Healthcare has operated as part of Optum Insight, UnitedHealth's data and technology analytics division, and its brand has been progressively folded into Optum's broader technology stack rather than run as an independently marketed product. The single most consequential event in the company's recent history is the February 2024 ransomware attack, which must be represented accurately and neutrally as a matter of historical record. On February 21, 2024, the ALPHV/BlackCat ransomware group gained unauthorized access to Change Healthcare's systems, reportedly after compromising a Citrix remote-access portal that lacked multi-factor authentication (initial access is believed to have occurred around February 12, 2024). The attack forced Change Healthcare to take systems offline, which cascaded into one of the most disruptive healthcare IT outages in US history: pharmacies could not verify insurance benefits, providers could not submit or track claims, remittance and payment processing stalled, and prior-authorization workflows broke down across thousands of hospitals, clinics, and pharmacies nationwide for weeks. UnitedHealth Group disclosed that the incident cost the company 872 million dollars in Q1 2024 alone, with a full-year 2024 impact estimated between 1.35 billion and 1.6 billion dollars, and it extended more than 9 billion dollars in interest-free loans and accelerated payments to affected providers by mid-2024 to ease cash-flow strain. The breach also exposed protected health information for a very large number of individuals, triggering federal investigations, congressional hearings, and numerous lawsuits. For an SEO directory audience researching Change Healthcare, the accurate picture is: a foundational, deeply embedded piece of US healthcare payment plumbing, now owned by Optum/UnitedHealth Group, that suffered a landmark cybersecurity incident in 2024 with lasting financial, regulatory, and reputational consequences, and that continues to operate today as an integrated part of Optum Insight's product portfolio.

long_form_blocks.heading: Company history and ownership
paragraphs: Change Healthcare's lineage is unusually layered for a company its size. Its earliest roots go back to 2007, when General Atlantic and Hellman and Friedman acquired the business-services segment of Emdeon (formerly WebMD's transaction-services arm), forming an independent claims-clearinghouse business. That business rebranded as Change Healthcare after acquiring a smaller patient-engagement company of the same name in 2015. In 2016 and 2017, McKesson Corporation combined the majority of its health information technology unit with Change Healthcare Holdings to form a new joint entity that kept the Change Healthcare name, with McKesson holding roughly a 70 percent stake. The combined company filed for an IPO in 2019 and traded publicly for a few years before being acquired.

In January 2021, Optum (UnitedHealth Group's health-services subsidiary) announced a deal to acquire Change Healthcare for approximately 13 billion dollars. The transaction faced a Department of Justice antitrust lawsuit over concerns that combining Optum and Change Healthcare would give UnitedHealth too much visibility into competitors' claims data; a federal judge rejected the DOJ's challenge in September 2022, and the acquisition closed on October 3, 2022. Since then, Change Healthcare has operated inside Optum Insight, UnitedHealth's data-and-analytics division, and its technology has been progressively integrated into Optum's broader platform rather than sold as a standalone, independently branded product.

long_form_blocks.heading: The February 2024 ransomware attack
paragraphs: On February 21, 2024, Change Healthcare confirmed a cyberattack attributed to the ALPHV/BlackCat ransomware group, with initial unauthorized access believed to have occurred around February 12, 2024 via a Citrix remote-access portal that reportedly lacked multi-factor authentication. Because Change Healthcare's clearinghouse handled an outsized share of US claims traffic (commonly cited at around half of all medical claims), taking its systems offline had an immediate, nationwide ripple effect: pharmacies could not run real-time benefit checks, providers could not submit or receive claim status updates, remittance advice stopped flowing, and prior-authorization requests backed up, forcing many practices and pharmacies onto manual, paper-based workarounds for weeks.

UnitedHealth Group's own SEC filings quantify the scale of the disruption. The company disclosed an 872 million dollar direct impact in the first quarter of 2024 alone, with guidance for a full-year impact in the 1.35 to 1.6 billion dollar range, and it extended more than 9 billion dollars in interest-free loans and accelerated funding to providers whose cash flow was disrupted by the outage. The incident also involved a large-scale data breach affecting protected health information, which led to regulatory scrutiny from HHS and the FTC, multiple congressional hearings, and a wave of class-action litigation. This event is one of the most significant healthcare cybersecurity incidents on record and is treated here as established historical fact rather than a current operational concern, since systems were substantially restored later in 2024.

features.title: Medical claims clearinghouse
features.description: Routes claims between healthcare providers and insurance payers, historically processing a very large share of all US medical claims traffic.

features.title: Eligibility and benefits verification
features.description: Real-time checks that let providers and pharmacies confirm a patient's insurance coverage and benefits before or at the point of care.

features.title: Payment and remittance processing
features.description: Electronic payment and remittance advice tools that move funds and reconciliation data between payers and providers.

features.title: Prior authorization workflows
features.description: Digital tools to submit, track, and manage prior-authorization requests required by insurers before certain treatments are approved.

features.title: Revenue cycle management
features.description: Software supporting the end-to-end billing lifecycle for hospitals and physician practices, from coding to collections.

features.title: Pharmacy network connectivity
features.description: Switches pharmacy claims and adjudication data between pharmacies, PBMs, and payers.

features.title: Healthcare data and analytics (Optum Insight)
features.description: As part of Optum Insight, Change Healthcare's data assets now feed into UnitedHealth's broader analytics, AI, and interoperability products.

features.title: Clinical and administrative interoperability tools
features.description: Connectivity infrastructure intended to help disparate provider and payer systems exchange administrative and clinical data.

pros: Extremely deep integration across US payers, providers, and pharmacies, giving broad claims coverage
Long operating history (roots back to 2007) with deep domain expertise in healthcare billing
Now backed by UnitedHealth Group and Optum's scale and capital resources
Wide range of revenue-cycle and interoperability products beyond just claims clearinghouse
cons: Suffered one of the most disruptive healthcare cyberattacks in US history in February 2024, exposing systemic fragility
The 2024 breach exposed protected health information, raising lasting privacy and trust concerns
Ownership by UnitedHealth/Optum raises antitrust and data-concentration concerns that were litigated by the DOJ
Not a self-serve or transparently priced product; access is enterprise-only through negotiated contracts
Brand is being absorbed into Optum Insight, making it harder to evaluate as a distinct standalone product

faqs.question: Is Change Healthcare still operating in 2026?
faqs.answer: Yes. Change Healthcare continues to operate as part of Optum Insight, the data and analytics division of UnitedHealth Group's Optum business, following the October 2022 acquisition. Its technology has been increasingly integrated into Optum's broader platform.

faqs.question: Who owns Change Healthcare?
faqs.answer: Change Healthcare is owned by Optum, a subsidiary of UnitedHealth Group. Optum completed its roughly 13 billion dollar acquisition of Change Healthcare on October 3, 2022, after a federal judge rejected a Department of Justice antitrust challenge to the deal.

faqs.question: What happened in the Change Healthcare cyberattack?
faqs.answer: On February 21, 2024, the ALPHV/BlackCat ransomware group breached Change Healthcare's systems, reportedly via a Citrix remote-access portal lacking multi-factor authentication. The company took systems offline, causing weeks of disruption to claims processing, benefits verification, and payments across the US healthcare system, and exposing protected health information for a large number of people.

faqs.question: How much did the 2024 attack cost UnitedHealth Group?
faqs.answer: UnitedHealth Group disclosed an 872 million dollar impact in Q1 2024 alone, with a full-year 2024 impact estimated between 1.35 billion and 1.6 billion dollars. The company also extended more than 9 billion dollars in interest-free loans and accelerated payments to providers affected by the outage.

faqs.question: Is Change Healthcare a SaaS product with public pricing?
faqs.answer: No. Change Healthcare is enterprise healthcare infrastructure sold through negotiated contracts with health systems, payers, and pharmacy networks. It does not publish self-serve pricing plans.

faqs.question: Was patient data compromised in the breach?
faqs.answer: Yes. The February 2024 incident involved unauthorized access to protected health information, which led to regulatory investigations, congressional hearings, and multiple lawsuits related to the scope of the data exposure.

faqs.question: Did the attack affect pharmacies and hospitals nationwide?
faqs.answer: Yes. Because Change Healthcare's clearinghouse handled a very large share of US claims and pharmacy transaction traffic, the outage disrupted benefits verification, claims submission, and payment processing at thousands of pharmacies, hospitals, and physician practices across the country for weeks.

faqs.question: What was Change Healthcare called before it had that name?
faqs.answer: Its lineage traces back to Emdeon's business-services segment, spun off in 2007. That business rebranded as Change Healthcare in 2015 and merged with McKesson's health IT unit in 2017 to form the company later acquired by Optum.

use_cases.title: Hospital and health system billing operations
use_cases.description: Revenue cycle and claims teams use Change Healthcare's clearinghouse and payment tools to submit claims, verify eligibility, and reconcile payments with insurers.
use_cases.audience: Hospital revenue cycle and billing departments

use_cases.title: Pharmacy claims adjudication
use_cases.description: Retail and specialty pharmacies route prescription claims through Change Healthcare's switch to confirm coverage and process payment with PBMs and insurers at the point of sale.
use_cases.audience: Retail and specialty pharmacy operators

use_cases.title: Payer-provider data exchange and interoperability
use_cases.description: Health insurers and provider organizations use Change Healthcare (now Optum Insight) infrastructure to exchange administrative and clinical data required for claims and prior authorization.
use_cases.audience: Health insurance payers and large provider networks

enrichment.short_description: Chartbeat is a real-time content analytics platform built for publishers and media companies, showing editors and audience-development teams what readers are doing on their site or app as it happens. It tracks concurrent visitors, engaged time, scroll depth, and traffic sources by article in real time, plus historical reporting, and is used by thousands of newsrooms worldwide to guide editorial and homepage decisions.
enrichment.pricing_model: Custom / quote-based subscription across tiered plans
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing based on pageviews; not publicly listed
enrichment.headquarters: Austin, Texas, United States (originally founded in New York City)
enrichment.seo_title: Chartbeat Review 2026 - Real-Time Analytics for Publishers and Newsrooms
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Chartbeat does, how its real-time newsroom analytics work, pricing structure, features, and how it compares for publishers and media companies.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chartbeat is a content intelligence and real-time analytics platform purpose-built for publishers, media companies, and newsrooms rather than general-purpose web analytics tools like Google Analytics. It was incubated inside Betaworks in New York City in 2009 and spun off as an independent company in 2010, quickly becoming known in newsrooms for its live dashboard showing exactly how many people are reading a given article right now, where they came from, and how engaged they are. The company's core differentiator is its focus on engaged time (how long readers actually spend actively reading, not just page load) rather than pure pageview counts, which newsroom editors use to make split-second decisions about what to promote on a homepage, push as a breaking alert, or feature in a newsletter. Chartbeat's product suite spans real-time dashboards for the newsroom floor, historical and predictive analytics for strategy teams, headline testing tools, and social referral tracking, all integrated via a lightweight JavaScript snippet added to a publisher's site or app. Chartbeat reports serving more than 5,000 media brands across 70-plus countries, ranging from large national newsrooms to smaller digital publishers, and it integrates with content management systems and ad-operations workflows common in the media industry. Ownership has changed over the years: Chartbeat operated independently for over a decade before being acquired by private equity firm Cuadrilla Capital in 2022, after which its operational headquarters shifted toward Austin, Texas, even though the brand remains strongly associated with its New York City media-industry roots. Pricing is not publicly published; Chartbeat sells through a sales-quote process with tiers commonly referred to as Essentials, Plus, Premium, and Enterprise, scaled to a publisher's pageview volume and feature needs (such as headline testing, deeper historical retention, or multi-brand reporting). For a directory audience, Chartbeat is best understood as a specialized, editorial-facing analytics tool rather than a general marketing or product analytics platform: its value proposition is speed and simplicity for non-technical newsroom staff making real-time content decisions, not deep funnel or conversion analysis.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Chartbeat does and who it is for
paragraphs: Chartbeat's flagship product is a real-time dashboard that newsroom editors, audience-development staff, and homepage producers watch throughout the day to see exactly which stories are gaining traction as they happen. Rather than the delayed, session-based reporting typical of general web analytics tools, Chartbeat shows concurrent active readers per article, live traffic-source breakdowns (search, social, referral, direct), and engaged-time metrics that estimate how much attention a piece of content is actually receiving. This real-time, editorial-first framing is why Chartbeat has remained a fixture in digital newsrooms for over a decade, even as broader analytics platforms have consolidated the martech landscape.

Beyond the live dashboard, Chartbeat offers historical and predictive reporting so strategy and SEO teams can analyze which topics, authors, or formats perform best over time, along with headline-testing tools that let editors A/B test different headlines on live traffic and automatically promote the winner. It also tracks how content performs across social platforms and search referral, which is central to how modern publishers plan distribution. Because the target user is often a non-technical editor rather than a data analyst, Chartbeat has invested heavily in a simple, glanceable interface rather than the flexible query-building found in general-purpose analytics suites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Company history and ownership
paragraphs: Chartbeat was founded in 2009 inside Betaworks, the New York City startup studio and incubator, and became an independent, venture-backed company in 2010. It built its reputation among digital-first newsrooms and legacy media companies alike during the 2010s as newsroom real-time dashboards became a standard fixture on editorial floors. In 2022, Chartbeat was acquired by private equity firm Cuadrilla Capital, a transition that shifted some of its corporate operations toward Austin, Texas, while the product and brand continue to be positioned squarely at the publishing and media industry that made it well known. Chartbeat says it now serves more than 5,000 media brands in over 70 countries, spanning large national newsrooms, digital-native publishers, and smaller regional outlets.

features.title: Real-time newsroom dashboard
features.description: Live view of concurrent readers, active headlines, and traffic sources updated continuously throughout the day.

features.title: Engaged-time measurement
features.description: Tracks actual reader attention rather than raw pageviews, helping editors gauge true content performance.

features.title: Headline A/B testing
features.description: Tests multiple headline variants on live traffic and can automatically promote the best-performing version.

features.title: Historical and predictive analytics
features.description: Longer-range reporting and forecasting tools for strategy, SEO, and audience-development teams.

features.title: Social and search referral tracking
features.description: Breaks down how content performs across social platforms, search engines, and other referral sources.

features.title: Homepage and editorial decision support
features.description: Purpose-built views for producers deciding what to promote, push as an alert, or feature editorially in real time.

features.title: Multi-brand and enterprise reporting
features.description: Enterprise plans support reporting across multiple publications or brands under one media organization.

features.title: Lightweight JavaScript integration
features.description: Installed via a simple tracking snippet added to a publisher's website or app, without heavy engineering lift.

pros: Purpose-built for newsroom workflows, far more intuitive for editors than general analytics tools
Real-time dashboard gives immediate feedback for time-sensitive editorial and homepage decisions
Engaged-time metric offers a more meaningful signal than raw pageviews for content quality
Long track record and large customer base of more than 5,000 media brands worldwide
Headline testing and social referral tools add practical audience-growth utility beyond basic reporting
cons: Pricing is not publicly disclosed, requiring a sales conversation to get a quote
Focused narrowly on media and publishing use cases, less suited to e-commerce or SaaS analytics needs
Costs scale with pageview volume, which can get expensive for high-traffic publishers
Some advanced features (like deeper historical data or headline testing) may be gated to higher tiers
Ownership change to a private equity firm in 2022 has made long-term product roadmap less publicly visible

faqs.question: What is Chartbeat used for?
faqs.answer: Chartbeat is a real-time content analytics platform used by publishers and newsrooms to see how readers are engaging with articles as it happens, helping editors decide what to promote, push, or feature editorially.

faqs.question: How much does Chartbeat cost?
faqs.answer: Chartbeat does not publish fixed pricing. It sells custom, quote-based plans (commonly referred to as Essentials, Plus, Premium, and Enterprise) scaled to a publisher's pageview volume and feature needs.

faqs.question: Who owns Chartbeat?
faqs.answer: Chartbeat was acquired by private equity firm Cuadrilla Capital in 2022, after operating independently since its 2010 spin-off from Betaworks.

faqs.question: When was Chartbeat founded?
faqs.answer: Chartbeat was founded in 2009 as a project within Betaworks in New York City and became an independent company in 2010.

faqs.question: How is Chartbeat different from Google Analytics?
faqs.answer: Chartbeat focuses on real-time, editorial-facing metrics like concurrent readers and engaged time on individual articles, designed for newsroom decision-making, whereas Google Analytics is a general-purpose, session-based analytics tool covering broader marketing and site-wide metrics.

faqs.question: Does Chartbeat work for non-media websites?
faqs.answer: Chartbeat is built and marketed specifically for publishers and media companies; it can technically be installed on other sites, but its features are optimized for editorial and content-driven use cases rather than e-commerce or SaaS analytics.

faqs.question: How many publishers use Chartbeat?
faqs.answer: Chartbeat states it partners with more than 5,000 media brands across over 70 countries.

faqs.question: Where is Chartbeat headquartered?
faqs.answer: Chartbeat was originally headquartered in New York City. Following its 2022 acquisition by Cuadrilla Capital, operations have also been associated with Austin, Texas.

use_cases.description: Newsroom producers monitor real-time engagement to decide which stories to promote on the homepage or push as breaking alerts.
use_cases.audience: Digital newsroom editors and homepage producers

enrichment.short_description: Chartboost is a mobile game advertising and monetization platform offering a demand-side platform, supply-side/mediation tools, and programmatic ad auctions for mobile app developers. Founded in 2011 by former Tapulous engineers, it was acquired by Zynga in 2021, came under Take-Two Interactive when Take-Two bought Zynga in 2022, and was then sold to adtech company LoopMe in December 2024, which now owns and operates it.
enrichment.pricing_model: Revenue share / programmatic ad platform (not a fixed-fee subscription)
enrichment.starting_price: Free to integrate; monetized via ad revenue share and programmatic bidding
enrichment.seo_title: Chartboost Review 2026 - Mobile Game Ad Network and Monetization Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What Chartboost is, how its mobile ad mediation and monetization platform works, its ownership history from Zynga to Take-Two to LoopMe, and key features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chartboost is a mobile advertising and monetization platform built specifically for mobile game developers, combining a demand-side platform (DSP), a supply-side platform (SSP), and mediation tools that let developers fill ad inventory through programmatic auctions across a large network of demand sources. It was founded in San Francisco in 2011 by Maria Alegre and Sean Fannan, both alumni of Tapulous (the studio behind Tap Tap Revenge, acquired by Disney in 2010), who set out to build a platform giving mobile game developers direct control over promoting, monetizing, and managing their games without relying solely on app-store discovery. Chartboost grew quickly in the 2010s, raising a 19 million dollar Series B led by Sequoia Capital in 2013 and expanding internationally, and it built a reputation as one of the most widely integrated mediation SDKs in mobile gaming, at its peak reporting a global audience of more than 700 million monthly users and tens of billions of monthly ad auctions. Ownership of Chartboost has changed hands multiple times in recent years, which is important context for anyone evaluating the platform today. In 2021, mobile games publisher Zynga acquired Chartboost for approximately 250 million dollars to strengthen its first-party advertising capabilities. When Take-Two Interactive completed its roughly 12.7 billion dollar acquisition of Zynga in May 2022, Chartboost came along as part of that deal and operated as an adtech division within Take-Two's mobile portfolio. In late 2024, Take-Two moved to divest Chartboost as it refocused on premium, AAA game development rather than mobile adtech; that divestiture was completed in December 2024, when adtech company LoopMe announced it had acquired Chartboost from Zynga (Take-Two), with the goal of using Chartboost's supply-side reach to extend LoopMe's brand-advertising business into more mobile apps. As of the most recent available information, Chartboost operates under LoopMe's ownership. For app developers, Chartboost remains primarily relevant as a mediation and monetization SDK integrated into games to serve interstitial, rewarded video, and banner ads, competing with platforms like AppLovin MAX, ironSource/Unity LevelPlay, and Google AdMob mediation.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Chartboost offers developers
paragraphs: At its core, Chartboost is an SDK that mobile game developers integrate into their apps to serve interstitial, rewarded-video, and banner ads, with a mediation layer that runs real-time auctions between multiple demand sources to maximize the price paid for each ad impression. On the demand side, Chartboost also operates its own DSP so advertisers (often other game studios looking to acquire new players through cross-promotion) can bid directly for inventory across Chartboost's publisher network. This dual-sided model, common among mobile adtech companies, lets Chartboost act simultaneously as a monetization partner for publishers and a user-acquisition channel for advertisers, historically processing tens of billions of programmatic auctions each month across a network of hundreds of millions of monthly active users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Ownership history: Zynga, Take-Two, and LoopMe
paragraphs: Chartboost's ownership has shifted twice in a few years. Zynga acquired Chartboost in 2021 for about 250 million dollars, aiming to bring first-party advertising infrastructure in-house to complement its game portfolio. Less than a year later, Take-Two Interactive Software agreed to acquire Zynga in a cash-and-stock deal valued at roughly 12.7 billion dollars, a transaction that closed in May 2022, bringing Chartboost under Take-Two's umbrella as an adtech division. By late 2024, reports emerged that Take-Two was in discussions to divest Chartboost so it could concentrate resources on premium console and PC game development rather than mobile advertising technology. That divestiture concluded in December 2024, when mobile brand-advertising company LoopMe announced its acquisition of Chartboost from Zynga/Take-Two, with terms not publicly disclosed. Under LoopMe, Chartboost is positioned to extend brand-advertising reach into mobile app inventory, a different strategic emphasis than its prior owners' focus on game-to-game user acquisition.

features.title: Mobile ad mediation
features.description: Real-time auction-based mediation layer that lets developers serve ads from multiple demand sources to maximize fill rate and revenue per impression.

features.title: Demand-side platform (DSP)
features.description: Lets advertisers, particularly game studios, bid programmatically for ad inventory across Chartboost's publisher network.

features.title: Supply-side platform (SSP)
features.description: Gives publishers programmatic access to a broad pool of advertiser demand for their ad inventory.

features.title: Rewarded video and interstitial ad formats
features.description: Supports common mobile ad formats used to monetize free-to-play games, including rewarded video, interstitials, and banners.

features.title: Cross-promotion tools
features.description: Historically offered developers a way to promote their own game portfolio to their existing player base at no cost.

features.title: Programmatic auctions at scale
features.description: Runs large volumes of real-time bidding auctions across its publisher and advertiser network.

features.title: Analytics and reporting dashboard
features.description: Gives developers visibility into ad performance, revenue, and fill rates across integrated placements.

features.title: SDK integration for major platforms
features.description: Provides SDKs for iOS and Android to integrate mediation and monetization into mobile games.

pros: Long track record in mobile game adtech dating back to 2011
Combines DSP, SSP, and mediation in one platform rather than requiring separate tools
Historically strong scale, with hundreds of millions of monthly active users across its network
Backed by larger corporate owners over time (Zynga, Take-Two, now LoopMe), providing resourcing and stability
Well-established SDK that many mobile game developers are already familiar with integrating
cons: Ownership has changed three times in under five years (Zynga to Take-Two to LoopMe), creating uncertainty about long-term product direction
Facing intense competition from larger, more dominant mediation platforms like AppLovin MAX and Unity LevelPlay
Not a transparent, published-pricing product; monetization economics depend on ad market conditions and revenue share
Primarily useful to mobile game developers, with limited relevance outside gaming adtech
Recent divestitures suggest it was viewed as non-core by its prior corporate parents

faqs.question: Who owns Chartboost now?
faqs.answer: As of the most recent available information, Chartboost is owned by LoopMe, a mobile brand-advertising company that acquired Chartboost from Zynga (a Take-Two Interactive label) in December 2024.

faqs.question: What does Chartboost do?
faqs.answer: Chartboost is a mobile advertising and monetization platform for game developers, combining ad mediation, a demand-side platform, and a supply-side platform to help apps serve ads and advertisers reach mobile game audiences programmatically.

faqs.question: When was Chartboost founded?
faqs.answer: Chartboost was founded in 2011 in San Francisco by Maria Alegre and Sean Fannan, both former Tapulous employees.

faqs.question: Was Chartboost owned by Zynga?
faqs.answer: Yes. Zynga acquired Chartboost in 2021 for approximately 250 million dollars. When Take-Two Interactive acquired Zynga in 2022, Chartboost came along as part of that deal.

faqs.question: Is Chartboost still owned by Take-Two Interactive?
faqs.answer: No. Take-Two divested Chartboost in December 2024, selling it to adtech company LoopMe.

faqs.question: How does Chartboost make money for developers?
faqs.answer: Chartboost operates on a revenue-share and programmatic-auction model: developers integrate its SDK to serve ads, and Chartboost's mediation layer runs auctions among demand sources to maximize the revenue paid per ad impression.

faqs.question: Is Chartboost free to use?
faqs.answer: Integrating the Chartboost SDK is free for developers; Chartboost generates revenue through its share of programmatic ad transactions rather than charging subscription fees.

faqs.question: How big is Chartboost's network?
faqs.answer: At its peak under Zynga ownership, Chartboost reported a global audience of more than 700 million monthly users and over 90 billion monthly advertising auctions, though current figures under LoopMe ownership have not been independently verified.

use_cases.title: Monetizing free-to-play mobile games
use_cases.description: Game developers integrate Chartboost's SDK to serve rewarded video, interstitial, and banner ads and generate revenue from their player base.
use_cases.audience: Mobile game developers and publishers

use_cases.title: User acquisition for mobile games
use_cases.description: Studios use Chartboost's demand-side platform to bid for ad placements across its publisher network to acquire new players.
use_cases.audience: Mobile game marketing and user-acquisition teams

use_cases.title: Brand advertising into mobile app inventory
use_cases.description: Under LoopMe ownership, brand advertisers can use Chartboost's supply reach to place campaigns across mobile game inventory.
use_cases.audience: Brand advertisers and media buyers targeting mobile audiences

enrichment.short_description: Chartio was a cloud-based business intelligence and data visualization tool that let non-technical users build dashboards and charts from SQL and NoSQL data sources without writing complex code. Atlassian acquired Chartio in February 2021, and rather than integrating it into its product suite, Atlassian discontinued the service entirely, shutting it down on March 1, 2022. Chartio is no longer available; this profile documents it as a discontinued, historical product.
enrichment.seo_title: Chartio Review 2026 - Discontinued BI Tool, Shut Down by Atlassian
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chartio was a business intelligence and data visualization platform acquired by Atlassian in 2021 and shut down in March 2022. Here is what it was and why it closed.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chartio was a cloud-based business intelligence (BI) and data visualization platform designed to let business users, analysts, and product teams build dashboards and explore data without needing to be expert SQL engineers, using a visual query builder alongside a native SQL interface. Founded around 2010 in San Francisco, Chartio built a reputation over roughly a decade as an accessible, mid-market BI tool that connected to a wide range of databases and data warehouses (such as PostgreSQL, MySQL, Snowflake, Redshift, and BigQuery) and let teams schedule reports, build interactive dashboards, and set up alerts, without the heavier implementation lift associated with enterprise BI suites like Tableau or Looker. It is critical to state clearly that Chartio no longer exists as an operating product. In February 2021, Atlassian announced it had acquired Chartio, initially framing the move as a step toward bringing cross-product analytics and visualization capabilities into the Atlassian ecosystem (Jira, Confluence, and related tools). However, Atlassian ultimately chose not to integrate Chartio's technology into its own suite and instead decided to wind the standalone product down entirely. Chartio's leadership communicated to existing customers that the platform would be discontinued, giving them roughly a year to migrate their dashboards and data connections elsewhere, and the service was officially shut down on March 1, 2022. Chartio's own CEO explained the decision as needing Atlassian to be singularly focused on its mission of delivering analytics and visualization specifically for the Atlassian platform, rather than continuing to operate Chartio as a general-purpose, multi-source BI tool for outside customers. As a result, anyone researching Chartio today, including for competitive or historical analysis, should treat it strictly as a discontinued, no-longer-available product. Former Chartio customers migrated to alternative BI platforms such as Looker, Metabase, Mode, Sigma Computing, or Reveal BI, several of which explicitly marketed themselves as Chartio replacements around the time of the shutdown. There is no current pricing, support, or product roadmap associated with Chartio, and the domain and brand are retained by Atlassian but not operated as an active service.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Chartio was before it shut down
paragraphs: While it was operating, Chartio positioned itself as an easier-to-adopt alternative to heavyweight enterprise BI tools. Its core interface combined a drag-and-drop visual query builder with a full SQL editor, so both non-technical business users and data-literate analysts could work in the same tool at their own comfort level. Users could connect Chartio to a wide range of relational and cloud data warehouses, build charts and dashboards, schedule automated report delivery, and share results across teams. It was commonly used by startups and mid-sized companies that needed self-service analytics without dedicating a full data engineering team to standing up and maintaining a BI stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Atlassian acquisition and shutdown
paragraphs: Atlassian announced its acquisition of Chartio in February 2021, describing it publicly as a move to strengthen Atlassian's own cross-product analytics and visualization capabilities rather than as an investment in growing Chartio as an independent, externally sold BI product. Over the following year, Atlassian made clear it would not continue operating Chartio for outside customers, and the company set a firm shutdown date of March 1, 2022, giving existing customers time to export their data and migrate to another BI platform. This outcome is an important, well-documented example of an acqui-hire-style acquisition in the data-tools space: rather than continuing or absorbing the acquired product, the acquiring company folded the team's expertise inward and discontinued the customer-facing service entirely. As of today, Chartio should be treated purely as a discontinued, historical product with no active subscription, support, or roadmap.

features.title: Visual and SQL query builder
features.description: Combined a drag-and-drop interface with a full SQL editor, letting both business users and analysts build queries in the same tool.

features.title: Broad data source connectivity
features.description: Connected to relational databases and cloud data warehouses such as PostgreSQL, MySQL, Snowflake, Redshift, and BigQuery.

features.title: Interactive dashboards
features.description: Let teams assemble multiple charts and visualizations into shareable, interactive dashboards.

features.title: Scheduled reporting and alerts
features.description: Supported automated report delivery on a schedule and threshold-based alerting on key metrics.

features.title: Data blending across sources
features.description: Allowed combining data from multiple connected sources into unified visualizations.

features.title: Team collaboration and sharing
features.description: Included permissions and sharing controls so dashboards could be distributed across an organization.

features.title: Embeddable charts
features.description: Offered options to embed charts and dashboards into other internal tools or applications.

features.title: Discontinued as of March 1, 2022
features.description: Atlassian shut the product down entirely after acquiring it in February 2021, and it is no longer available in any form.

pros: Was historically praised for its approachable, dual visual/SQL interface for mixed technical-skill teams (no longer relevant since shutdown)
Had broad connectivity across common relational databases and cloud data warehouses (historical)
Was a lower-friction alternative to enterprise BI suites for smaller teams (historical)
cons: Completely discontinued and unavailable since March 1, 2022; cannot be purchased, signed up for, or used today
Existing customers were forced to migrate all dashboards and data connections to a different BI tool within about a year
Atlassian did not integrate Chartio's technology into any surviving public product, so the acquisition delivered no continuity for customers
Any modern search results or listings suggesting Chartio is active are outdated or inaccurate

faqs.question: Is Chartio still available?
faqs.answer: No. Chartio was shut down by Atlassian on March 1, 2022, roughly a year after Atlassian acquired the company in February 2021. It cannot be signed up for or used today.

faqs.question: Why did Atlassian shut down Chartio?
faqs.answer: Atlassian acquired Chartio in February 2021 but chose not to integrate it into its own product suite. Chartio's CEO said the decision reflected a need for Atlassian to focus specifically on delivering analytics and visualization for the Atlassian platform, rather than continuing to run Chartio as a general-purpose external BI product.

faqs.question: What can I use instead of Chartio?
faqs.answer: Former Chartio users migrated to alternative business intelligence platforms such as Looker, Metabase, Mode, Sigma Computing, and Reveal BI, several of which marketed themselves directly as Chartio replacements around the time of the shutdown.

faqs.question: When did Atlassian acquire Chartio?
faqs.answer: Atlassian announced its acquisition of Chartio in February 2021.

faqs.question: What did Chartio do before it shut down?
faqs.answer: Chartio was a cloud-based business intelligence and data visualization tool that let users connect to databases and data warehouses, build dashboards using either a visual query builder or SQL, and share reports across teams.

faqs.question: Can I still access old Chartio dashboards or data?
faqs.answer: No. Since the service was shut down on March 1, 2022, Chartio's infrastructure is no longer operating, and any dashboards or data not migrated elsewhere before the shutdown are not accessible.

faqs.question: Was Chartio integrated into Atlassian's products like Jira or Confluence?
faqs.answer: No. Despite early framing suggesting Chartio's technology might inform Atlassian's analytics capabilities, Chartio was not integrated into Jira, Confluence, or any other surviving Atlassian product; the standalone service was discontinued instead.

use_cases.title: Historical reference for BI tool comparisons
use_cases.description: Analysts researching the business intelligence market may reference Chartio as a historical example of a discontinued mid-market BI product.
use_cases.audience: Industry researchers and BI tool evaluators

use_cases.title: Migration planning context for former Chartio customers
use_cases.description: Former Chartio users researching where their data and workflows went can use this profile to confirm the shutdown timeline before selecting a replacement tool.
use_cases.audience: Former Chartio customers evaluating replacement BI platforms

enrichment.short_description: Chatterino is a free, open-source, third-party Twitch chat client for Windows, macOS, and Linux, built to be faster and more customizable than Twitch's built-in web chat. Developed in the open on GitHub by a community of contributors, it supports multiple simultaneous channel tabs, third-party emote services like BetterTTV, FrankerFaceZ, and 7TV, chat filtering and highlighting, and extensive UI customization.
enrichment.seo_title: Chatterino Review 2026 - Free Open-Source Twitch Chat Client
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chatterino is a free, open-source Twitch chat client with multi-channel tabs, third-party emotes, and deep customization. See features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chatterino is a free, open-source desktop chat client built specifically for Twitch, designed as a faster, more feature-rich, and more customizable alternative to Twitch's native web-based chat panel. The current, actively maintained version, commonly referred to as Chatterino 2, is developed openly on GitHub by a small team of maintainers (notably the developers known as fourtf and pajlada) along with community contributors, and it is distributed free of charge for Windows, macOS, Linux, and BSD. Its core appeal to Twitch viewers, moderators, and streamers is performance and control: Chatterino is built to stay smooth and responsive even when a user has many high-traffic channels open simultaneously in separate tabs or split views, which is a common pain point with Twitch's own web chat during large streams or raids. Beyond raw performance, Chatterino integrates support for the major third-party Twitch emote services, including BetterTTV (BTTV), FrankerFaceZ (FFZ), and 7TV, so users see the full range of community emotes that Twitch's default chat does not natively render. It also offers granular customization: users can build custom highlight rules for keywords, usernames, or specific message patterns, set up filters to hide unwanted messages (such as spam or bot commands), adjust themes and fonts, and rearrange the interface to fit their workflow, whether they are a casual viewer, a moderator managing multiple channels, or a streamer monitoring their own chat alongside others. Chatterino also includes built-in commands for common moderation and utility actions, such as blocking or unblocking users, viewing chatter counts, and clearing messages, without needing to type raw Twitch IRC commands. Because it is open source, Chatterino's code is publicly auditable on GitHub, and it has no subscription, ads, or paid tiers; it is maintained through volunteer development and community contributions rather than a commercial business model. Chatterino has become one of the most widely used third-party Twitch chat clients within the streaming community, particularly among viewers and moderators of high-volume channels who need a lighter, faster, more customizable alternative to the native Twitch chat experience.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Chatterino is and how it works
paragraphs: Chatterino is a standalone desktop application that connects to Twitch's chat infrastructure independently of the twitch.tv website, giving users a dedicated, lightweight window (or set of windows) purely for chat. Users log in with their Twitch account and can then open any number of channels as separate tabs, split panes within a single window, or entirely separate popped-out windows, which is especially useful for moderators or viewers who follow many streams at once. Because it is written with performance as a priority, Chatterino is designed to handle very high message throughput, such as during major esports or gaming events with tens of thousands of concurrent chatters, without the lag or dropped messages that can occur in a browser tab running Twitch's native chat.

long_form_blocks.heading: Open-source, community-driven development
paragraphs: Unlike commercial chat tools, Chatterino is developed entirely in the open on GitHub under an open-source license, with its codebase, issue tracker, and roadmap publicly visible. This means updates, bug fixes, and new features are driven by a combination of core maintainers and community pull requests, and the tool has no company behind it selling subscriptions or collecting user data for commercial purposes. This model has helped Chatterino build trust within the Twitch community as a genuinely free tool, and it has enabled rapid support for evolving Twitch platform features and third-party emote services over the years.

features.title: Multi-channel chat management
features.description: Open and manage many Twitch channels simultaneously via tabs, split views, or separate popped-out windows.

features.title: Third-party emote support
features.description: Renders emotes from BetterTTV, FrankerFaceZ, and 7TV in addition to native Twitch emotes.

features.title: Custom highlights and notifications
features.description: Set up highlight rules for specific keywords, usernames, or message patterns to catch important messages.

features.title: Message filtering
features.description: Hide unwanted messages, such as spam or bot output, using configurable filters.

features.title: Deep UI customization
features.description: Adjust themes, fonts, layout, and window arrangement to match a user's workflow.

features.title: Built-in moderation and utility commands
features.description: Includes shortcuts for common actions like blocking or unblocking users, clearing chat, and viewing chatter counts.

features.title: Lightweight, high-performance client
features.description: Optimized to stay responsive even with many high-traffic channels open at once.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Available for Windows, macOS, Linux, and BSD.

pros: Completely free with no ads, subscriptions, or paid tiers
Open-source codebase is publicly auditable, building trust within the streaming community
Handles high-volume, multi-channel chat far more smoothly than Twitch's native web chat
Extensive support for third-party emote services that Twitch's own chat does not render
Highly customizable highlighting, filtering, and layout options for power users and moderators
cons: Requires a separate desktop install rather than working directly in the browser like native Twitch chat
Customization depth can be overwhelming for casual users who just want simple chat
Being community-maintained, feature requests and bug fixes depend on volunteer contributor availability
Does not include native video playback, so it is used alongside, not instead of, watching the stream
Occasional compatibility lag when Twitch changes its underlying chat APIs, until maintainers can update

faqs.question: Is Chatterino free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chatterino is completely free and open source, with no subscriptions, ads, or paid tiers.

faqs.question: Does Chatterino support BetterTTV and 7TV emotes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chatterino natively integrates third-party emote services including BetterTTV (BTTV), FrankerFaceZ (FFZ), and 7TV, alongside standard Twitch emotes.

faqs.question: What platforms does Chatterino run on?
faqs.answer: Chatterino is available for Windows, macOS, Linux, and BSD.

faqs.question: Is Chatterino safe and official?
faqs.answer: Chatterino is not an official Twitch product, but it is a well-established, open-source third-party client with publicly auditable code on GitHub and a long track record within the Twitch streaming community.

faqs.question: Can I watch the Twitch stream inside Chatterino?
faqs.answer: No. Chatterino focuses purely on chat functionality; users typically run it alongside a browser or the Twitch app to watch the actual video stream.

faqs.question: Why do streamers and moderators use Chatterino instead of Twitch's built-in chat?
faqs.answer: Chatterino offers better performance during high-traffic streams, multi-channel management, custom highlights and filters, and support for third-party emotes, all of which the native Twitch chat lacks.

faqs.question: Who develops Chatterino?
faqs.answer: Chatterino is developed openly on GitHub by a small team of maintainers along with community contributors, rather than by a commercial company.

faqs.question: Does Chatterino require a Twitch account?
faqs.answer: Yes. Users log in with their existing Twitch account credentials to send messages and access channels, the same as they would through Twitch's own chat.

use_cases.title: Managing multiple Twitch channels at once
use_cases.description: Moderators and viewers who follow many streams simultaneously use Chatterino's tabbed and split-view layout to keep track of several chats in one place.
use_cases.audience: Twitch moderators and multi-channel viewers

use_cases.title: Following high-traffic streams smoothly
use_cases.description: Viewers of large streams or esports events use Chatterino for a faster, less laggy chat experience than Twitch's native web chat during peak traffic.
use_cases.audience: Viewers of high-volume Twitch channels and events

use_cases.title: Custom highlighting for streamers
use_cases.description: Streamers and their moderators set up custom highlight rules to catch important messages, such as subscriptions, mentions, or moderator alerts, amid a fast-moving chat.
use_cases.audience: Twitch streamers and their moderation teams

enrichment.short_description: Chatty is a free, open-source desktop chat client for Twitch, written in Java, offering an IRC-style interface with Twitch-specific features. It supports joining multiple channels in tabs or separate windows, streamer tools like updating stream title and game from the client, emote autocomplete, and multi-language support, and runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux wherever Java is supported.
enrichment.company_size: Open-source community project (independent developer plus community contributors)
enrichment.seo_title: Chatty Review 2026 - Free Java-Based Twitch Chat Client
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Chatty is a free, open-source Java desktop chat client for Twitch with IRC-style features, streamer tools, and multi-language support. See features and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Chatty is a free, open-source desktop chat client purpose-built for Twitch, written in Java, that gives viewers, moderators, and streamers an IRC-inspired chat experience with a range of Twitch-specific enhancements layered on top. Because it is built in Java, Chatty runs cross-platform on any system with a compatible Java runtime, including Windows, macOS, and Linux, and it is distributed in multiple packaged forms, including a portable version bundled with its own Java runtime environment so users without Java already installed can still run it. Chatty's interface lets users join numerous Twitch channels at once, organized as tabs, split views, or separate popped-out windows, which suits both casual viewers following several streams and moderators or streamers who need to keep an eye on multiple chats simultaneously. What sets Chatty apart from a purely generic IRC client is its deep set of Twitch-aware conveniences: streamers can update their stream title, category or game, and tags directly from within the chat client, and can even trigger ad commercials using a configurable hotkey, all without switching over to the Twitch dashboard in a browser. For viewers, Chatty provides emote autocomplete, the ability to locally hide specific emotes they don't want to see, and support for standard emoji shortcodes. The tool has also been localized by its community into several languages, including German, French, Russian, and Japanese, in addition to English, reflecting a long-running, internationally active user base. Chatty has existed as a free, community-maintained project for many years, predating some of its more recently developed alternatives, and it remains actively referenced in Twitch tool round-ups and creator resource guides as a lightweight, no-cost option for anyone who wants more control and fewer distractions than Twitch's native web chat, without necessarily needing every advanced customization feature found in some newer third-party clients. It has no company, subscription, or paid tier behind it; the software is free to download from its official GitHub-hosted project pages.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Chatty is and who it is for
paragraphs: Chatty is a desktop application, not a browser extension or web app, that connects directly to Twitch's chat servers to give users a dedicated, low-distraction chat window separate from the twitch.tv site. Because it is written in Java, it is genuinely cross-platform, and the project offers download packages both for users who already have a Java runtime installed and for those who prefer a self-contained portable version that includes the runtime bundled in. This makes Chatty an accessible option for viewers on older or lower-powered machines who want a lighter alternative to running a full browser tab just for chat.

long_form_blocks.heading: Streamer-focused tools and localization
paragraphs: A notable differentiator for Chatty is that it is not purely a viewer-facing chat client: it includes tools aimed at streamers themselves, letting them change their stream title, game or category, and tags, and even trigger ad commercials, directly from within the chat interface using hotkeys, without needing to alternate between Chatty and the Twitch creator dashboard in a browser. Combined with moderation-friendly features like joining several channels in separate tabs or windows and community-driven translations into languages such as German, French, Russian, and Japanese, Chatty has built a loyal niche following among Twitch users, particularly those who prefer a classic IRC-client feel over more modern, heavily skinned chat applications.

features.title: Multi-channel chat in tabs or windows
features.description: Join multiple Twitch channels simultaneously, organized as tabs, split views, or separate popped-out windows.

features.title: In-client stream management for streamers
features.description: Update stream title, game/category, and tags, and trigger ad commercials via hotkey, without leaving the chat client.

features.title: Emote autocomplete and local emote hiding
features.description: Autocompletes emote names while typing and lets users locally hide specific emotes they do not want to see.

features.title: Emoji shortcode support
features.description: Recognizes standard emoji shortcodes like colon-thinking-colon alongside Twitch emotes.

features.title: IRC-style interface
features.description: Offers a classic IRC-client look and feel with advanced options familiar to long-time chat client users.

features.title: Multi-language interface
features.description: Community translations support English, German, French, Russian, Japanese, and other languages.

features.title: Portable, no-install option
features.description: Available as a portable package bundled with its own Java runtime, so it can run without a separate Java install.

features.title: Free and open source
features.description: Distributed at no cost with publicly available source code, with no subscription or paid tier.

pros: Completely free with no subscription, ads, or paid tiers
Genuinely cross-platform via Java, running on Windows, macOS, and Linux
Unique in-client tools for streamers to update title, game, and tags without a browser
Long-running, mature project with community translations into several languages
Portable version removes the need to separately install a Java runtime
cons: Java-based architecture and IRC-style interface can feel dated compared to newer, more modern-looking clients
Less actively marketed and may be less well known than alternatives like Chatterino
Requires a Java runtime unless using the bundled portable package, adding setup friction for some users
Third-party emote service support and customization depth may lag behind newer competing clients
As a community-maintained tool, update cadence depends on volunteer developer availability

faqs.question: What is Chatty?
faqs.answer: Chatty is a free, open-source Twitch chat client written in Java, offering an IRC-style desktop chat experience with Twitch-specific features for both viewers and streamers.

faqs.question: Is Chatty free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chatty is free and open source, with no subscription fees or paid tiers.

faqs.question: What platforms does Chatty support?
faqs.answer: Because it is built in Java, Chatty runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, and any other operating system that supports Java 8 or later.

faqs.question: Can streamers manage their stream from Chatty?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chatty lets streamers update their stream title, game or category, and tags, and even trigger ad commercials with a hotkey, directly from within the chat client.

faqs.question: Do I need to install Java separately to use Chatty?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Chatty offers a portable download that bundles its own Java runtime environment, so users without Java already installed can still run it.

faqs.question: What languages does Chatty support?
faqs.answer: Chatty's interface has been translated by its community into several languages, including English, German, French, Russian, and Japanese.

faqs.question: How is Chatty different from Chatterino?
faqs.answer: Both are free, open-source third-party Twitch chat clients, but Chatty is built in Java with an IRC-style interface and unique in-client streamer management tools, while Chatterino is written in C++ with a more modern interface and broader third-party emote-service integration.

faqs.question: Where can I download Chatty?
faqs.answer: Chatty is distributed through its official project pages hosted on GitHub, where users can find both standard and portable downloads.

use_cases.audience: Twitch viewers following multiple channels

use_cases.audience: Twitch streamers managing their own broadcasts

use_cases.description: Users on older or lower-powered machines choose Chatty for a lighter-weight alternative to running Twitch chat in a full browser tab.

enrichment.short_description: Checkbot is a Chrome and Edge browser extension for technical SEO, broken link checking, and website auditing, letting developers and SEOs crawl hundreds of pages at once to catch issues like broken links, duplicate titles, missing meta tags, invalid HTML/CSS/JavaScript, and mobile-friendliness problems. Built by independent developer Sean Wilson, it offers a free tier for unlimited small-site checks plus a paid Pro tier with deeper crawl and export features.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (free tier plus paid Pro subscription)
enrichment.starting_price: Free; paid Pro plans available (exact pricing set by developer, billed quarterly or yearly)
enrichment.seo_title: Checkbot Review 2026 - Browser-Based SEO and Broken Link Checker Extension
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Checkbot is a Chrome/Edge extension for SEO audits, broken link checking, and site crawling. See features, pricing, pros, cons, and FAQs for developers and SEOs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Checkbot is a browser extension for Google Chrome and Microsoft Edge that gives web developers, technical SEOs, and site owners a fast way to audit a website for common SEO, performance, and security issues directly from the browser, without needing to set up a separate crawling service or install desktop software. It was created by Sean Wilson, an independent web app developer based in Edinburgh, UK, who built the tool out of a personal collection of testing utilities he had accumulated while consulting on web projects, aiming to automate away tedious, error-prone manual checks that developers and SEOs otherwise had to perform by hand. Once installed, Checkbot can crawl hundreds of pages on a website simultaneously, checking for more than 50 common problems based on best practices recommended by organizations like Google and Mozilla. Its SEO-focused checks include a broken link checker, analysis of page titles, meta descriptions, and headings, detection of duplicate content and duplicate titles, validation of canonical URLs, checks for missing or unoptimized image ALT tags, robots.txt validation, redirect chain analysis, and identification of 404 and other error pages. Beyond core SEO, Checkbot also validates HTML, CSS, and JavaScript for errors, flags deprecated web platform features, checks for mobile-friendliness, and runs basic website security checks (such as insecure content or missing security headers), making it a broader website health tool rather than a narrowly scoped SEO checker. Checkbot uses a freemium model: the free version allows unlimited checks on small websites at any frequency, which is enough for many individual site owners or small-business audits. The paid Checkbot Pro tier unlocks features aimed at professional and agency users, including the ability to crawl localhost or password-protected staging sites, generate PDF audit reports for clients, export full crawl results to CSV for further analysis, customize crawl settings and depth, and check sites sitting behind HTTP authentication. With more than 80,000 users on the Chrome Web Store and a 4.9-out-of-5 star rating, Checkbot has built a loyal following among freelance developers, in-house web teams, and technical SEOs who want a lightweight, in-browser alternative to heavier desktop crawlers like Screaming Frog for quick, ad hoc site audits.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Checkbot checks and how it works
paragraphs: Checkbot runs directly inside the Chrome or Edge browser as an extension, which means there is no separate application to install or server-side crawler to configure. Once activated on a site, it crawls hundreds of pages in parallel, checking each against more than 50 rules covering SEO, HTML/CSS/JavaScript validity, mobile-friendliness, and basic security posture. On the SEO side specifically, it flags broken internal and external links, missing or duplicate page titles and meta descriptions, heading structure issues, canonical URL problems, unoptimized image ALT attributes, robots.txt misconfigurations, redirect chains, and 404 or other error responses, giving users a prioritized list of issues to fix directly in the browser rather than requiring them to export data to a separate report first.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing model and who uses it
paragraphs: Checkbot follows a freemium model built around a genuinely usable free tier: the free version supports unlimited checks on small websites, with no artificial time limits on how often a site can be re-crawled. This makes it accessible to solo developers, bloggers, or small-business owners who just want an occasional health check on their own site. The paid Checkbot Pro tier is aimed at professionals and agencies who need to check larger or more complex sites, work with localhost or staging environments protected by HTTP authentication, export raw crawl data to CSV for deeper analysis, generate polished PDF reports to share with clients, and customize crawl behavior (such as depth, page limits, or included/excluded URL patterns). Because it was built and is maintained by a single independent developer, Sean Wilson, rather than a large SEO software company, Checkbot has positioned itself as a lightweight, no-frills, highly focused alternative to larger enterprise SEO platforms, appealing especially to developers who want quick, in-browser answers rather than a full marketing-suite subscription.

features.title: Large-scale in-browser site crawling
features.description: Crawls hundreds of pages simultaneously directly from the Chrome or Edge extension, without separate crawler software.

features.title: Broken link checking
features.description: Identifies broken internal and external links across the crawled pages of a website.

features.title: SEO metadata analysis
features.description: Checks page titles, meta descriptions, headings, canonical tags, and duplicate content across the site.

features.title: HTML, CSS, and JavaScript validation
features.description: Flags invalid or deprecated HTML, CSS, and JavaScript based on web standards best practices.

features.title: Mobile-friendliness and security checks
features.description: Tests pages for mobile usability issues and basic security concerns such as insecure content or missing headers.

features.title: Redirect and error page detection
features.description: Identifies redirect chains, 404 errors, and other problematic HTTP response codes.

features.title: PDF and CSV export (Pro)
features.description: Paid Pro tier lets users generate client-ready PDF audit reports and export full crawl results to CSV.

features.title: Localhost and HTTP-auth site support (Pro)
features.description: Pro tier allows crawling of localhost development sites and sites protected by HTTP Basic Authentication.

pros: Free tier offers genuinely unlimited checks on small websites with no time restrictions
Runs directly in the browser with no separate software install required
Covers more than 50 checks spanning SEO, code validation, mobile-friendliness, and basic security in one tool
Highly rated by users, with around 80,000 Chrome Web Store users and a 4.9-star rating
Fast, ad hoc auditing workflow well suited to developers who want quick answers without a full SEO platform subscription
cons: As a browser extension, it is inherently limited to what can be crawled and processed within the browser environment, unlike dedicated desktop crawlers for very large sites
Built and maintained by a single independent developer, which may raise concerns about long-term support compared to larger SEO software vendors
Pro-tier pricing and feature gating require checking the current checkbot.io site, as it is not consistently published across third-party listings
Lacks the broader marketing suite features (keyword research, backlink analysis, rank tracking) found in larger all-in-one SEO platforms
Best suited to technical users comfortable interpreting raw crawl and validation output rather than marketers wanting simplified dashboards

faqs.question: What is Checkbot used for?
faqs.answer: Checkbot is a Chrome and Edge browser extension used to audit websites for SEO issues, broken links, HTML/CSS/JavaScript errors, mobile-friendliness problems, and basic security concerns by crawling hundreds of pages at once.

faqs.question: Is Checkbot free?
faqs.answer: Checkbot has a free tier that allows unlimited checks on small websites at any frequency. A paid Checkbot Pro tier adds features like localhost crawling, PDF reports, CSV export, and custom crawl settings.

faqs.question: Who created Checkbot?
faqs.answer: Checkbot was created by Sean Wilson, an independent web app developer based in Edinburgh, UK, who built it from a personal toolkit of website-testing utilities.

faqs.question: Does Checkbot work on localhost or staging sites?
faqs.answer: Checking localhost sites and sites protected by HTTP authentication is a paid Checkbot Pro feature, not included in the free tier.

faqs.question: How many checks does Checkbot perform?
faqs.answer: Checkbot crawls websites and checks for more than 50 common problems based on best practices recommended by Google and Mozilla, covering SEO, code validity, mobile-friendliness, and security.

faqs.question: Is Checkbot available for browsers other than Chrome?
faqs.answer: Checkbot is distributed as an extension for Google Chrome and Microsoft Edge, both of which are Chromium-based browsers.

faqs.question: How does Checkbot compare to Screaming Frog?
faqs.answer: Checkbot is a lighter-weight, browser-based alternative to desktop crawlers like Screaming Frog, well suited to quick, ad hoc audits rather than very large-scale enterprise crawls.

faqs.question: Can Checkbot generate reports for clients?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only on the paid Pro tier, which includes the ability to generate PDF audit reports and export crawl results to CSV.

use_cases.title: Quick technical SEO audits
use_cases.description: Developers and SEOs use Checkbot to quickly crawl a site and catch broken links, duplicate titles, and metadata issues without setting up a separate crawling tool.
use_cases.audience: Technical SEOs and web developers

use_cases.title: Pre-launch website QA
use_cases.description: Teams run Checkbot against staging or localhost sites (on the Pro tier) to catch broken links, invalid code, and SEO issues before a site goes live.
use_cases.audience: Web development and QA teams

use_cases.title: Client site audits for freelancers and agencies
use_cases.description: Freelance developers and small agencies use Checkbot Pro to generate PDF audit reports and CSV exports for client website health checks.
